Thunderstorms with hail up to 1 inch in diameter and 40 to 50 mph winds were reported near Boulevard and Tierra Del Sol. These thunderstorms were most likely pulse severe type storms. There were no reports of injuries or damage.

Thunderstorms with hail up to 1 inch in diameter and 40 to 50 mph winds were reported near Boulevard and Tierra Del Sol. These thunderstorms were most likely pulse severe type storms. There were no reports of injuries or damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tamarack Fire was lightning initiated on July 9th and located 14 miles north of Camp Nelson in the Sequoia National Park. The fire was initially battled but became a resource burn in early August. There were no structures lost nor were there casualties to fight the fire. The total cost to battle the fire reached $879,000 by the end of August and 4,654 acres had been burned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning sparked two large wildfires in the rugged terrain between San Gorgonio and Yucca Valley. The Sawtooth Fire burned 61,700 acres and caused considerable damage in the historic movie town of Pioneertown. It was in Pioneertown where the fire killed one man, damaged 12 residences, and destroyed 50 homes, 191 cars and trucks, 3 R.V.s, 27 trailers, 2 railcars, and 9 tractors. Meanwhile, the Millard Fire burned an estimated 24,000 acres of remote, mountainous terrain in and near the San Gorgonio Wilderness. The two fires merged on the 14th in the Mission Creek Drainage area east of San Gorgonio. The entire fire became known as the Sawtooth-Millard-Heart Complex and all combined, burned roughly 85,700 acres.
A 40 ft tall Jacaranda tree was blown over during a thunderstorm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm along the Elsinore Convergence Zone produced a landspout tornado near Menifee. The landspout blew over about a dozen pine trees and a few palm trees at the Menifee Lakes Country Club. A few homes were damage by falling trees.

The Cottonwood Fire burned approximately 2,346 acres around Sorrell Peak in the Piute Mountains of Kern County about 15 miles south of Weldon. The cause of the fire was unknown but 2 vehicles, a few outbuildings, and some trailers were destroyed in the first day of the blaze. One injury occurred in fighting the fire. The cost to fight the fire was estimated at $1.03 million.
California Highway Patrol reported flash flooding in the community of Lancaster from Avenue K to the Kern County border.
A severe thunderstorm produced damaging winds in the community of Lancaster. Local law enforcement reported trees blown down at the intersection of Avenue J and 20th street due to thunderstorm winds.

California Highway Patrol reported flash flooding and debris flow in the mountains of Los Angeles county, near the intersection of Big Rock Creek and Big Pines Road. Several cars were stuck in the mud and debris.

The Burnt Fire was lightning initiated late in the afternoon of the 21st of July about 8 miles north of Cedar Grove in the Fresno County portion of the Southern Sierra Nevada. The fire was immediately allowed to burn as a resource management fire and continued through September. The total acreage burned was only 627 acres by the end of September.

A steady north wind of 30 knots and several gusts to 40 knots occurred over San Luis Reservoir in West Merced County from late in the afternoon on the 8th through midnight. Elevated wind speeds continued through the very early morning hours of the 9th.

The Broder-Beck Fire was lightning initiated July 25th and became a resource management burn through the month of August. The fire was located in the Sequoia National Forest about 48 miles northeast of Porterville and burned 3,490 acres by September 19th. The cost to battle the fire to August 25th was assessed at $560,276. No structures were lost nor were there any casualties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large dome of High Pressure brought hot conditions to the SF Bay Shoreline - with a moist southeast flow keeping temperatures unusually warm overnight. High temperatures reached as high as 103 degrees with low temperatures at night only falling into the lower 70s.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Frog Complex Fire was a lightning initiated fire on July 21st but became a resource management fire. The fire was located 1 mile West of Laurel Lake or 19 miles northwest of Yosemite Village. The fire continued through August and September and burned 5,578 acres by October 2nd and at a cost of $850,033.
Heavy rainfall caused flash flooding 5 miles east of Walker.  Wildland firefighters reported that some livestock were lost.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heat advisory conditions settled over the North Bay Valleys on July 21st and persisted for 5 days. High temperatures ranged from 108 to 113 and elevated humidity levels at night kept low temperatures in the 70s. One fatality was reported in Napa County.

Heat Advisory conditions were met for several days in the North Bay Mountains - as a huge dome of High Pressure brought hot and somewhat humid conditions to the region. Temperatures at 1700ft reached 105 during the day and held in the upper 70s at night.

Heat advisory conditions were experienced in the East Bay Valleys for several days in late August. Temperatures soared to as high as 112 degrees in some locations, with overnight lows holding in the mid and upper 70s. Several reports of heat related illness were reported.

CZ_NAME: MOUNTAINS OF SAN BENITO COUNTY AND INTERIOR MONTEREY COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense heat settled over the mountain areas of San Benito County - with high temperatures soaring to as high as 115 degrees one day. Temperatures were over 100 for at least 15 consecutive days in mid/late July, and low temperatures were unusually elevated - holding mainly in the mid 60s.

CZ_NAME: EAST BAY HILLS AND THE DIABLO RANGE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense heat settled over the East Bay Hills during this 5 day stretch in August. Temperatures at Mt.Diablo soared to over 100 degrees for 5 consecutive days with lows hovering near 80.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several mesonet stations near the town of Independence recorded wind gusts in excess of 50 knots during a mountain wave/downslope windstorm event.

The King Fire located 2 miles northeast of Mr. Starr King in Yosemite National Park was lightning initiated on July 19th and was maintained as a resource burn through August. The fire burned 157 acres through September as a resource burn with a cost to manage assessed at $23,000 by the end of August.

The Boundary Fire located 1.5 miles northwest of El Capitan near Yosemite Village was a human caused fire that burned 536 acres. The fire cost $2.1 million to fight but no structures were lost nor did any casualties occur.

The Middle-T Fire was a lightning initiated fire on July 20th located 10 miles northwest of Yosemite Village. The fire was initially battled but became a resource management burn over 451 acres that had cost $1.1 million to battle through August 27th. There were no structures lost nor were there any injuries.

The Roaring Fire was lightning initiated on July 22nd and located one mile south of Cedar Grove in Kings Canyon National Park. The fire became a resource management burn through August and burned 1525 acres by the end of September. No structures were lost nor were there any injuries. The cost to battle the fire was not known.

CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN SALINAS VALLEY / ARROYO SECO / LAKE SAN ANTONIO
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning induced a wildfire near San Ardo in Southern Monterey County on July 22nd. The fire burned until controlled on August 1st after a total of 14,507 acres was consumed. No known injuries or deaths were reported - and only 1 outbuilding was destroyed.

CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN SALINAS VALLEY / ARROYO SECO / LAKE SAN ANTONIO
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures settled over the Southern Salinas Valley for several days in late July. High temperatures soared to over 110 degrees in many locations, and overnight lows stayed in the middle 60s for several nights.

CZ_NAME: SANTA CLARA VALLEY INCLUDING SAN JOSE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot weather settled upon the Santa Clara Valley - yielding an extended period of high temperatures over 100 degrees and lows in the 70s. Some areas in the Southern Santa Clara Valley reached 115 degrees during the day and fell only to around 80 at night. One death was reported in San Jose.

A large dome of High Pressure combined with a moist southeast flow aloft to bring very hot and somewhat sticky conditions to the Santa Cruz Mountains. Temperatures at the summit reached around 110 degrees and nighttime lows fell only to around 70.

An early July spell of high maximum temperatures occurred from the 8th through the 10th of the month when the warmest parts of the Central and Southern San Joaquin Valley had temperatures spike above 105 degrees F. in the warmest areas. Fresno reached 107F on the 9th and 10th, Bakersfield climbed to 105F on those two dates, and Merced reached 106F on the 9th.

An intense thunderstorm pushed northwestward through the Antelope Valley and into the Kern County portion of the Mojave Desert the late afternoon of the 20th. Initially strong wind caused damage by felling trees and power poles, and the high wind even blowing out windows on cars as it crossed the desert. Numerous lightning strikes occurred in its passage across the Mojave Desert and as it extended into the Tehachapi area of the Kern County Mountains. Locally heavy rain was reported in the Lancaster-Palmdale area of northern Los Angeles County with the storm's passage.

A thunderstorm pushed through the southwest portion of Yosemite National Park in the afternoon of the 20th. High wind felled trees that knocked out power at El Portal due to a tree over a power line at 1600 PDT.  Trees were subsequently downed across the Wawona Highway about 10 miles north of the Park's South Entrance by 1630 PDT.

Over several days from the 20th of the month through the 24th, locally heavy rain occurred from strong afternoon thunderstorm development in the Southern Sierra Nevada. On the 23rd heavy rain in the vicinity of Huntington Lake resulted in mud and debris over the Highway 168 one mile west of the Sierra Summit Ski Resort with Big Creek Road below Huntington Lake also affected by mud and debris over the roadway at the same time.

A small fire around Keyesville near Lake Isabella Dam in the Kern County Mountains was likely set by a lightning strike in the evening of the 20th. Due to the evening thunderstorm activity a number of other small wildfires were set in the Kern County Mountains. These fires included the Clear Fire, located near Eagle Peak in the Piute Mountains (5 acres burned); the Rock Fire south of Heald's Peak near Kelso Valley (one acre burned); and, the Scodie Fire in Scodie Canyon (two acres burned).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fire was lightning initiated in the evening of the 19th near Gibonney Creek north of Onyx east of Lake Isabella that burned in excess of 100 acres by the 20th. No further information was available.
Minor property damage.
Nearly a foot of water over some roads.
At Red Pass Lake mesonet station.
Flooding closed U.S. Highway 95 near Needles.  Several cars stuck in the water.
More than a foot of water over Ghost Town Road.
Recorded at Cow Creek air quality station 4 miles north of Furnace Creek.

CZ_NAME: SAN JOAQUIN T NW/STANISLAUS/X SW TUOLUMNE/SW CALAVERAS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low temperatures this morning were chilly for a date in August.  The low temperature at the Stockton Municipal Airport was 50 degrees, which broke the previous record for the date of 51 degrees set in 1955.  At the Sacramento Executive Airport, the low temperature this morning was 51 degrees.  This tied the previous record for the date also set in 1955.
A late night shower produced a rare rainfall event for early August in Red Bluff when 0.01 inches of rain fell at the airport.  This breaks the old daily record of a trace set in 1945.

Thunderstorms along the Elsinore Convergence Zone caused significant mudslides on Soboba Road between Lake Park Drive and Gilman Springs Road. At least 19 vehicles were trapped due to 8 separate mudslides triggered by heavy rain. One man was trapped in his vehicle for two hours before he was rescued. A few homes and businesses nearby were affected or damaged by the mudslides. The flash flooding and mudslides occurred in an area affected by both the Soboba Fire (2005) and the Golf Fire (2006).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning sparked two small fires along highway 79 northwest of Warner Springs near Sunshine Summit. The fires burned four acres before they were extinguished.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms rapidly formed at the intersection of the Elsinore Convergence Zone and the Banning Pass Arc/Convergence Zone near San Jacinto and Hemet. A microburst caused damage to trees, buildings, and power lines from San Jacinto to Temecula and heavy rain caused flash flooding and mudslides. Spotters reported trees down and wind damage to buildings in and around Hemet. Several roads in Hemet had to be closed due to flash flooding and a half-dozen cars stalled in deep water at the Warren Road rail crossing. Further east, mudslides were reported along highway 74 in the foothills. An advertising balloon that was blown into power lines in Menifee sent burning streamers onto a bed of wood chips and started a small fire. In Temecula, extensive minor damage to tents and sheds occurred at the Wilson Creek Winery while a roof was ripped off an outbuilding at the Longshadow Ranch Winery. Damage to both Temecula wineries is estimated at about 20K.

Severe thunderstorms rapidly formed at the intersection of the Elsinore Convergence Zone and the Banning Pass Arc/Convergence Zone near San Jacinto and Hemet. A microburst caused damage to trees, buildings, and power lines from San Jacinto to Temecula and heavy rain caused flash flooding and mudslides. Spotters reported trees down and wind damage to buildings in and around Hemet. Several roads in Hemet had to be closed due to flash flooding and a half-dozen cars stalled in deep water at the Warren Road rail crossing. Further east, mudslides were reported along highway 74 in the foothills. An advertising balloon that was blown into power lines in Menifee sent burning streamers onto a bed of wood chips and started a small fire. In Temecula, extensive minor damage to tents and sheds occurred at the Wilson Creek Winery while a roof was ripped off an outbuilding at the Longshadow Ranch Winery. Damage to both Temecula wineries is estimated at about 20K.

A severe thunderstorm north of Santa Rosa Mountain produced heavy rain, localized flash flooding, and damaging wind gusts across the eastern slopes of the Riverside County Mountains and western Coachella Valley. A rain gage at Dead Indian Canyon measured 1.14 inches of rain in 30 minutes and 1.30 inches in 45 minutes. Rain gages at Agave Hill and Haystack Mountain reported 1.18 and 0.87 inches of rain respectively during a similar time frame. Flash flooding was observed in and around the rural community of Pinyon Flats. Several vehicles were trapped in a mudslide 2 feet deep on highway 74 and several dirt roads in Pinyon Pines were heavily eroded. It did not appear that any homes in Pinyon Pines sustained damage. Outflow from the storm raced toward the Coachella Valley which resulted in downed palm fronds and power lines, broken power poles, and damaged street signs. The downed power lines caused power outages that affected 3,000 Southern California Edison customers from Palm Springs to Cathedral City.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser observed nickel size hail and measured a wind gust of 47 mph from a thunderstorm south of Morena Village. Frequent cloud to ground lightning was also observed by a resident of Campo.
A thunderstorm wind gust brought down power lines and power poles along Briggs Road at Chambers Road and at Matthews Road. Power was knocked out and traffic was disrupted in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Santa Ana wind event occurred across portions of Southern California. The strongest winds affected the mountains and valleys of Ventura and Los Angeles counties. Scattered power outages were reported across the area due to downed power lines. In Ventura county near the community of Moorpark, the gusty offshore winds helped to spread the Shekell wildfire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Shekell Fire burned 13,600 acres around the community of Moorpark in Ventura county. The suspected cause of the fire was downed power lines near the MGR Design manufacuring plant. The gusty Santa Ana winds spread the fire quickly. Five homes were destroyed and five others were heavily damaged. The fire also destroyed the MGR Design manufacturing plant. Two minor injuries were reported when a fire engine overturned on a secondary road.

CZ_NAME: MONTEREY BAY/N SALINAS VALLEY/HOLLISTER VALLEY/CARMEL VALLEY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An 8.1 earthquake was reported at 0314 PST on November 15th, 2006. During the afternoon and early evening a series of surge waves moved into Northern and Central California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A series of surge waves from a distant earthquake caused approximately 8 hours of jostling in Santa Cruz Harbor. About a 4 foot range of tidal difference occurred causing damage to 7 docks.

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure over the Great Basin resulted in gusty Santa Ana Winds across Southern California. The wind was particularly strong in San Bernardino. At Palm Avenue Elementary School, the wind gusted over 75 mph seven times with a peak gust of 92 mph at 6:39 AM on the 3rd. Downed power lines sparked a small fire that burned less than an acre of land. The wind was also quite strong in the Santa Ana Mountains and foothills. Fremont Canyon RAWS measured numerous wind gusts greater than 60 mph, including a peak gust of 75 mph between 8:30 and 9:30 AM. While most of San Diego County escaped the worst of the wind, there were still isolated reports of damage, including 16 downed power poles along Valley Center Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage to wires on a power pole sparked a 100 acre wildfire in Norco. No structures were damaged and no injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low clouds behind a Pacific storm system pushed into northward- and westward-facing slopes of the Southern Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains on the morning of the 22nd. Visibilities below 1/4 mile affected travel on Highway 58 and Interstate 5. Added moisture to the floor of the Central and Southern San Joaquin Valley also resulted in dense fog during the night and morning hours from the 23rd through the 25th, with visibilities as low as 200 feet.

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Offshore flow in the wake of a passing trough of low pressure caused gusty Santa Ana winds in the Inland Empire. The strong winds downed trees and power lines in the usual areas, and overturned a big rig on Interstate 15 in Fontana. One downed power line sparked a small fire in Devore. A wind gust of 56 mph was measured nearby in Rialto.

Lightning struck a palm tree in a San Bernardino neighborhood. The lightning bolt traveled down the palm tree, split the tree in two, and then traveled into the ground where it damaged some gas and water lines. The thunder was severe enough to break the windows in three of the surrounding homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning caused damage to three homes in San Bernardino.

CZ_NAME: COASTAL NORTH BAY INCLUDING POINT REYES NATIONAL SEASHORE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system swept across the San Francisco and Monterey Bay Areas, knocking out power to thousands of homes and businesses. One person died when gusty winds knocked a tree into a home in Lagunitas, in Marin County. Numerous roadways were closed after the blustery winds swept debris onto them. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An 8.3 magnitude earthquake off the Kuril Islands produced a tsunami that propagated across the Pacific Ocean and impacted the West Coast of North America, including California.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tsunami surges affected Crescent City Harbor beginning at 1138PST and lasting for several hours.  The peak wave height was 5.8 feet as measured trough to peak. No injuries or deaths resulted from this tsunami. Damage was limited to docks and boats in Crescent City Harbor. The damage was caused by the high velocity currents resulting from the tsunami. No other significant wave heights occurred elsewhere in Del Norte County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another Pacific storm moved onshore this day...and this time cold air was already in place in the valleys.   Snow levels dropped to unseasonably low levels as this storm moved through.  Initially, a Heavy Snow Warning was issued for California zones CAZ080/081/082/083 above 3000 feet at 25/1509 PST, effective from 26/1300 until 27/1000 PST.  CAZ081 was downgraded to a Snow Advisory at 26/0323 PST, and the Warning was changed to a Winter Storm Warning over the remaining zones due to expected strong winds.  This new Warning was effective from 26/1000 until 26/2200 PST, after which it was downgraded to a Snow Advisory.  A few observations verified the heavy snow;  they are listed here.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This observation was from a SNOTEL at Sand Flat, elevation 6750 feet.  This is above warning level for WFO Medford criteria, but it is usually indicative of conditions below the station and above the snow level.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Freeze Warning was issued for California zone CAZ080 at 11/0242 PST, effective from issuance until 11/0800 PST.  Recorded low temperatures ranged from 25 to 37 degrees, so it definitely verified over parts of the zone.

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong offshore pressure gradient in combination with upper level support from a trough over the Intermountain West caused a period of gusty Santa Ana Winds across portions of Southern California. The strongest winds were observed in the Inland Empire as well as in and below passes and canyons of the Santa Ana Mountains. Fremont Canyon RAWS measured a wind gust of 73 mph, and later measured a sustained wind of 54 mph. Elsewhere, Ontario International Airport reported a 58 mph wind gust and blowing dust. Between noon and 4 PM, Caltrans responded to 100 calls regarding downed street signs, trees, and power lines. There were even a few reports of overturned big rigs. In Highland, a Eucalyptus Tree fell through an upstairs apartment, temporarily trapping the residents. In Mira Loma, the wind blew roof shingles off homes, snapped tree branches, and blew over a light pole at an apartment complex. In Orange County, an estimated 15,000 people lost power during the high winds. Damage to tree branches and roof shingles was reported in Costa Mesa and a 40-foot palm tree fell onto a 2002 Mercedes Benz in Santa Ana.

Following a dry, cold-frontal passage through Interior Central California brisk northerly wind aloft resulted in widespread gusty wind over the Kern County Mountains and Deserts on Monday the 16th. Wind gusted to over 40 knots over higher terrain in the Kern County Mountains and Kern Deserts.

Closed low pressure aloft brought widespread precipitation to the Southwest United States including the Kern County Mountains and Desert from late Friday the 13th through much of Saturday the 14th. With the locally heavy rain roads were flooded as culverts clogged. Much of the rain fell in the early morning hours of the 14th when Democrat, in the mountains, received 0.96 of rain and Laural Mountain, in the desert, got 0.65 of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front that caused gale force winds over the coastal waters brought strong winds across much of coastal Orange County, San Diego County, and portions of the Riverside and San Bernardino County mountains and deserts. The winds were most widespread right along the San Diego coast where wind gusts exceeded 40 mph in many locations. A few gusts even exceeded 50 mph, including 54 mph at La Jolla, 52 mph at Torrey Pines, and 51 mph at Point Loma. While wind gusts were generally less than 40 mph in most other areas, a few locations did manage gusts in excess of 40 mph, including 49 mph at Camp Pendleton Target Range and Huntington Beach, 47 mph at Borrego Springs, 45 mph at Ranchita and Victorville, and 41 mph at Bell Canyon. There was only one location that officially observed gusts meeting High Wind critera, and that was Burn's Canyon with a wind gust of 69 mph early that morning. Most of the damage reports came from San Diego County where numerous downed trees and power lines were sighted. Several vehicles across the county were damaged by falling trees.

A Pacific storm moved across the Central California interior the evening of the 26th and morning of the 27th. Storm-total snowfall amounts reached as high as 19 inches at Poison Ridge (elevation 6900 feet msl). Ice on the road closed Interstate 5 through the Kern County mountains. A very tight surface pressure gradient developed over the Indian Wells Valley behind the storm on the 28th, triggering gusty north to northeast winds. State Highway 58 was closed after a camper was blown off a truck. State Highway 14 was also closed after 2 trucks were blown over near Mojave (Kern County).

An approaching Pacific storm system produced strong south winds ahead of a cold front that moved across the Central and Southern San Joaquin Valley on the 8th. Gusts up to 39 mph raised dust from plowed farmland, reducing visibility to near zero at times. At least one traffic accident was attributed to the event when a pickup rear-ended a tractor-trailor on State Highway 99 near Tulare (Tulare County). Winds also resulted in arcing powerlines that sparked a power-pole fire west of Hanford (Kings County). Fresno (Fresno County) reached a record high temperature for December of 77 degrees due to downslope winds from the Sierra Nevada foothills. The storm itself moved across the Southern Sierra Nevada from the evening of the 9th to the morning of the 10th. Snowfall amounts were generally 13-17 inches, with one location reaching 23 inches (Poison Ridge, elevation 6900 feet msl).

During the morning of October 22, distant storms caused waves to affect the coast of Humboldt County that had a significant height of seven feet and a dominant period of sixteen seconds. These wave conditions produced sneaker waves, which are infrequent waves that are much higher than the rest of the waves occurring during a period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A sneaker wave knocked down and pulled into the ocean a 4-year-old girl. Witnesses reported that the girl was high on the dry sand when the unusually large sneaker wave occurred. A 21-year-old women attempted to rescue the girl and both the girl and the woman died in the surf.

A strong surface high over Idaho continued to bring an unseasonably cold Canadian airmass to the Central California Interior in the wake of a late-November storm. Many locations in the Central and Southern San Joaquin Valley fell into the 20s on the morning of the 1st. Sub-freezing temperatures continued in parts of the valley through the 8th. The coldest reading was 24 degrees at Hanford (Kings County) on the 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Record daily high temperatures were set in the Northern San Joaquin Valley under high pressure and clear afternoon skies.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A new daily high temperature record of 70 degrees was set in Modesto on December 8th.  This breaks the old record of 69 degrees set in 1939.|A new daily high temperature record of 69 degrees was set in Stockton on December 10th.  This breaks the old record of 67 degrees set in 2004.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two fires started by lightning in July burned into October.  The last report on these fires was dated October 19th.  At that time, the Bar Complex was 100,414 acres and was 84% contained.  The estimated containment date was 10/25/06, and 61.2 million dollars was spent on fire fighting efforts.   The Uncles was 30,424 acres and was 95 percent contained.  The estimated containment date was 10/31/06, and 15.5 million dollars was spent on fire fighting efforts.

A Pacific storm rolled in on this date and brought snow to the mountains of northern California.  A Snow Advisory was issued for this system at 12/1859 PST, expiring at 13/0600 PST.  Most of the reports verified the advisory, but a few reports listed here met Heavy Snow Warning criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sand Flat SNOTEL at 6800 feet reported 8 inches of snow in this interval.  This elevation is above normal warning levels, but it does give some idea of conditions below.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In late December, a fast-moving storm system moved through Southern California. The storm did not produce any significant rainfall, but did generate a severe thunderstorm with large hail in Sherman Oaks. The main weather associated with this storm system was the gusty offshore winds that developed behind the storm. The gusty winds affected Santa Barbara, Ventura and Los Angeles counties and gusted as high as 100 MPH. Widespread power outages were reported across the area due to downed power lines. The winds also damamged numerous orhcards across Santa Barbara and Ventura counties, resulting in over $15 million dollars of crop damage. In Lake Casitas, the winds damaged a large boat dock, scattering numerous boats across the lake. One injury due to a falling tree was reported at a campground in Santa Barbara county. Large ocean waves also occurred during this event and resulted in the death of one man who was trying to save two family members who were swept into the ocean by the large waves. The two family members swept into the ocean were eventually saved.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One man drowned off of Ventura Beach, trying to save his neice and grandneice who were swept off a jetty by large waves. The neice and grandneice were eventually saved.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At Northstar-at-Tahoe Ski Resort, total snowfall of 14 inches was reported at an elevation of 8300 feet with 10 inches reported at 6900 feet.

Strong surface high pressure over Idaho brought an unseasonably cold airmass to the central California interior. Sub-freezing temperatures occurred in the Central and Southern San Joaquin Valley late in the evening of the 28th and continued in some areas as late as December 8th. The coldest reading was 25 degrees in Hanford (Kings County) on the morning of the 29th. Cold weather extended into the first widespread killing frost of the season for the Kern Deserts on the morning of the 30th.

In late December, a fast-moving storm system moved through Southern California. The storm did not produce any significant rainfall, but did generate a severe thunderstorm with large hail in Sherman Oaks. The main weather associated with this storm system was the gusty offshore winds that developed behind the storm. The gusty winds affected Santa Barbara, Ventura and Los Angeles counties and gusted as high as 100 MPH. Widespread power outages were reported across the area due to downed power lines. The winds also damamged numerous orhcards across Santa Barbara and Ventura counties, resulting in over $15 million dollars of crop damage. In Lake Casitas, the winds damaged a large boat dock, scattering numerous boats across the lake. One injury due to a falling tree was reported at a campground in Santa Barbara county. Large ocean waves also occurred during this event and resulted in the death of one man who was trying to save two family members who were swept into the ocean by the large waves. The two family members swept into the ocean were eventually saved.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rose Valley RAWS sensor reported a wind gust of 62 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low moving over Southern California generated scattered showers and thunderstorms. On the night of the 13th, a strong thunderstorm with very heavy rain and hail formed over the city of San Bernardino. A gage at Cal State San Bernardino recorded 0.51 inches just within the first 5 minutes of the deluge. By the time 30 minutes had passed, 1.81 inches of rain had collected in the gage and a flash flood had been triggered. Approximately 18 homes and businesses and 2 vehicles were damaged by the flooding. The water undermined Electric Road and left a hole 8 feet wide, 75 feet long, and 4 feet deep. One man was rescued from the top of his car as water rushed over his hood. The flash flood rushed through homes and deposited appliances and furniture out in the street. When the flood water receded, the streets were covered in mud and boulders. There were no injuries reported.
